text: The patient is on spontaneous ventilation with a laryngeal mask. An incision is made below the xiphoid process of the sternum, and the linea alba is incised. The xiphoid process is identified, and blunt dissection is performed under the sternum using the surgeon's finger. Local anesthetic is administered. A skyhook is positioned to elevate the sternum, creating space for surgery in the mediastinum. Thoracoscopic instruments are introduced for dissection under the sternum, progressing into the right pleural cavity.

The surgeon incises the right mediastinal pleura off the pericardium using a ligature, staying close to the right phrenic nerve to minimize residual fatty tissue. A second incision is made on the mediastinal pleura behind the sternum, medial to the right internal mammary artery, and connected to the first incision. The thymus is dissected from the back of the sternum using the ligature and retraction with a metal sucker. The dissection continues from the medial border of the superior vena cava (SVC) to the innominate vein, focusing on the right superior horn of the thymus. The thymus is then separated from the anterior face of the pericardium and the SVC.

The surgeon continues dissection, identifying the left innominate vein and dividing the thymic veins leading to it using a Ligasure device. Dissection progresses over the innominate artery towards the trachea. Local anesthetic is injected near the right phrenic nerve to reduce diaphragm movement. Standard diathermy is employed to access the left pleural space. Positive pressure is administered through a laryngeal mask. Dissection continues upwards along the sternum using the left internal mammary artery as a landmark, creating a second plane towards the left phrenic nerve while maintaining a safe margin. The thymus is dissected off the anterior pericardium, preserving fatty tissue in the sample, and branches from the left internal mammary artery and vein are divided.

The surgeon dissects the left superior horn of the thymus from the left innominate vein using a ligature device, which efficiently divides small vessels and fatty tissue with minimal thermal spread. The thyrothymic ligament is identified at the neck, marking the superior margin of the thymus. The final portion of thymic tissue is separated from the left innominate vein, completing the excision of the thymus from the patient's chest.

The excised thymus is displayed next to a 20 ml syringe for size comparison. The subxiphoid approach provides a clear view of both pleural spaces and the anterior mediastinum. The two lungs are visible on either side, with the pericardium centrally located. Above the pericardium, the left innominate vein is inspected for residual tissue. Below the vein, the aortic arch and brachiocephalic artery are visible, with the trachea situated further back. The chest wall movements indicate the patient is breathing spontaneously.

The surgeon begins skin closure after positioning a drain tube linked to a digital chest drainage system with suction. The tube is removed due to no air leak. The surgical wound measures approximately four centimeters in length. A chest X-ray in recovery shows a slightly raised right diaphragm due to the local anesthetic block. A subsequent X-ray on the first post-operative day shows full lung expansion and no pleural effusion.
